options.name: Boolean flag soup (Firewatch-minimal)
options.summary: Named booleans set at choice points; dialogue branches check flags directly. The default pattern in Ink/Twine-scale games.
options.pros: Trivial to implement and debug; zero query layer; fastest possible path to a playable slice; writers already understand it.
options.cons: No metadata (who knows, when, how bad); per-character knowledge needs a flag-per-event-per-character; irrecoverable data loss when you later want reactions you didn't anticipate; combinatorics live in the writer's head; gossip and the knowledge-based mystery are nearly unbuildable.

options.name: Raw affinity counters (classic reputation)
options.summary: Per-character trust/warmth/fear numbers mutated directly by choices; dialogue gates on thresholds.
options.pros: Cheap, legible, easy to tune; produces smooth tone shifts; players intuitively understand meters.
options.cons: Lossy — identical scores from opposite histories are indistinguishable; can only ever produce generic reactions, never specific callbacks ('you left him at the bridge'); rebalancing weights breaks existing saves; directly contradicts the 'remembers what you DID' pillar.

options.name: Append-only event ledger + query layer
options.summary: Every meaningful action appended as an immutable event; all reactions are queries over history; axes computed from events.
options.pros: Lossless — late-development ending conditions can query anything that ever happened; retroactive rebalancing via re-derivation; pure/replayable, ideal for the shared web+terminal core and golden-log regression tests.
options.cons: Without a knowledge layer, NPCs implicitly know everything in the log — dramatic irony, secrets, and gossip need bolting on; raw events are not writer-friendly without a DSL and tooling.

options.name: Witnessed-facts + derived axes + salience rules (recommended)
options.summary: Fact ledger (what happened) + per-character Witness sets (who knows it, from whom, how distorted) + relationship axes derived purely from known facts + Left 4 Dead-style specificity-scored dialog rules with guaranteed fallbacks + a gossip propagation pass at scene transitions.
options.pros: Gossip, secrets, provenance ('who told you?'), confession-vs-discovery, and the central mystery are all first-class knowledge-set operations; specificity ranking kills combinatorial explosion (author only the cases you care about); derived axes give CK3-style explainability and save-safe rebalancing; pure TS module fits both builds and enables soak-test simulation.
options.cons: Highest upfront cost of the deterministic options: needs the query API, writer DSL/compiler, and three internal tools (why-this-line debugger, coverage linter, simulator) before writers are productive; gossip pacing needs real tuning or the world feels either omniscient or deaf.

options.name: LLM-summarized memory (rolling character 'impressions')
options.summary: Feed each character's event history to an LLM that maintains a natural-language impression of the player, consulted or generated at dialogue time.
options.pros: Infinite surface variety; gracefully handles unanticipated combinations; on-trend.
options.cons: Non-deterministic — breaks replay, testing, and fair-play mystery clueing; runtime cost/latency and an online dependency the terminal build can't assume; voice drift off the authored characters; directly conflicts with the brief's 'authored/deterministic, LLM limited' requirement. Acceptable only offline as an authoring-assist (drafting candidate callback lines for writers to curate).

topPick: Option 4 — Witnessed-facts + derived axes + salience rules. It is the only architecture where the game's two pillars are the same mechanism: "characters remember what you did" and "there is a hidden truth clued in retrospect" are both knowledge-set operations over an immutable fact ledger. The salience-scored rule matcher (proven in Left 4 Dead, Firewatch, and spiritually in Hades' prerequisite pool) solves the authoring explosion — writers add only the specific reactions they want and specificity ranking plus guaranteed fallbacks handle the rest. Derived (never stored) axes give CK3-grade explainability, save-compatible rebalancing, and honor the immutability standard, while the pure seeded TypeScript core drops unchanged into both the browser and terminal builds and makes thousand-run soak testing trivial. Build the ledger + knowledge sets + rule matcher + fallback ladder first; add gossip propagation and distortion in a second pass once one character's greeting slot proves the loop.
risks: 1) Tooling-before-content dependency: writers are unproductive until the DSL, why-this-line debugger, and coverage linter exist — budget these into the first milestone or the whole memory pillar slips. 2) Gossip tuning: too fast and NPCs feel omniscient (player agency around secrets evaporates), too slow and the system is invisible; needs a knowledge-flow visualization and playtest iteration; decide early whether the player can ever permanently contain a fact. 3) Forgiveness model is a creative decision the engine can't make: do axis penalties decay (CK-style half-lives), require reparative facts, or last forever? This defines the game's moral tone. 4) QA of emergent combinations: salience matching means untested rule interactions; mitigate with the soak simulator and golden-log replays, but accept some weirdness budget. 5) Fair-play mystery discipline: knowledge sets make the twist representable but not automatically fair — need a manual 'clue audit' pass mapping each shard to ≥2 in-world foreshadows (including motif placement). 6) Localization: templated callback lines with fact params complicate translation; freeze the param vocabulary early. 7) Cast size creep: every added character multiplies gossip pairs and rule coverage; hold the line at 5. 8) Open question: should the debug 'why does she feel this way' view ship to players in some diegetic form (journal, ending recap)? CK3 suggests transparency deepens attachment; a mystery game may prefer opacity until the reveal.
